ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for stir-fried spicy rice noodle with vegetables; FOODON_00002187 (noodle dish) used as the closest broader class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (stir-fried noodle dish) used as closest defensible analogue; no exact code for spicy rice noodle stir-fry with vegetables in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to this specific prepared dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: mixed vegetables (bean sprouts, cabbage, bell pepper, carrot)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of stir-fried spicy rice noodle with vegetables weighs approximately 450 g, as recorded from the GrabFood menu listing for this dish.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (HPB Foodo / Singapore Food Composition Database)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from the HPB Singapore food composition database using the closest analogous entry for stir-fried rice noodle dishes (bee hoon goreng / fried rice vermicelli). The input record's per-100g values (energy 144 kcal, protein 3.3 g, fat 6.2 g, carbohydrate 18.9 g) are consistent with HPB reference ranges for fried bee hoon dishes and have been retained with medium confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, vitamins A, B1, B2, B3, B6, B12, folate, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water are not reported in the HPB entry for this dish category and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — Rice noodles / rice vermicelli analogous entry
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific stir-fried spicy rice noodle dish. GI of 61 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice noodles / rice vermicelli (GI ~61, medium category; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). The stir-frying process, added fat, and vegetables modestly lower the effective GI relative to plain boiled rice noodles (GI ~58–65 range). GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (61 × 85.0) / 100 = 51.9 ≈ 52.1 g (carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 18.89 × 4.5 = 85.0 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg per 100 g; a full 450 g plate delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, representing 78–90% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and soy sauce during preparation, limit portion size to no more than 200 g, and pair with potassium-rich foods to partially offset the high sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice noodles are refined high-GI carbohydrates (GI ~61); a 450 g plate delivers ~85 g carbohydrate with only ~6 g dietary fibre, producing a high glycaemic load (~52) that drives significant post-meal glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, request extra vegetables to increase fibre and reduce the noodle-to-vegetable ratio, and ask for reduced sugar in the sauce to lower the overall glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 400 mg per 100 g and saturated fat at 1.78 g per 100 g are the primary cardiovascular risk factors; trans fat is low at 0.04 g per 100 g; cholesterol is 11.11 mg per 100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size to reduce cumulative sodium and saturated fat intake, request cooking oil reduction, and choose a version with increased vegetables to improve the fibre and micronutrient profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Curry paste provides anti-inflammatory curcumin and capsaicin; however, refined rice noodles contribute to high glycaemic load which may drive low-grade systemic inflammation, and sodium at 400 mg per 100 g may worsen RA-related tissue oedema.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade vegetables (chilli, bell pepper) should monitor symptoms; requesting reduced sauce and extra vegetables will lower sodium and glycaemic load while preserving anti-inflammatory spice benefits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Spicy Rice Noodle with Vegetables (also known as Spicy Bee Hoon or Mee Hoon Goreng Pedas) is a popular Singaporean hawker dish featuring thin rice vermicelli noodles wok-fried at high heat with a spicy curry-infused or chilli-based sauce, mixed vegetables such as bean sprouts, cabbage, carrot, and bell pepper, egg, and seasoned with fish sauce and soy sauce. The dish has a characteristic smoky wok-hei aroma, a tender-chewy noodle texture, and a bold spicy-savoury flavour profile with mild sweetness from the sauce. It is commonly sold at hawker centres and food courts across Singapore and is influenced by both Malay and Thai culinary traditions, reflecting the multicultural food heritage of the region. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 144 kcal, 3.3 g protein, 6.2 g fat, and 18.9 g carbohydrate with 1.3 g dietary fibre and 400 mg sodium. The high sodium content (400 mg per 100 g) makes it a concern for individuals with hypertension, while the refined rice noodle base with a medium glycaemic index of approximately 61 and high glycaemic load per serving (~52) makes it unsuitable for uncontrolled type 2 diabetes without portion modification. The dish is halal-certified in most hawker settings and is dairy-free. Vitamin C content of 5.6 mg per 100 g is contributed by the vegetables and chilli. The dish is best consumed in moderate portions with additional vegetables to improve the fibre and micronutrient density.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from the HPB Singapore food composition database using the closest analogous fried bee hoon / rice vermicelli dish entry. The input record's per-100g values are consistent with HPB reference ranges and have been retained. No exact HPB entry for this specific named dish (614D Stir-fried Spicy Rice Noodle with V) was found; values are therefore classified as medium confidence. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for rice noodles.
notes: 1. Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database using the closest analogous fried rice noodle (bee hoon goreng) entry; no exact match for this specific dish code (614D) was found in the HPB database. Values retained from input record at medium confidence as they are consistent with HPB reference ranges for this dish category. 2. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, vitamin A, vitamin B1 (thiamine), vitamin B2 (riboflavin), vitamin B3 (niacin), vitamin B6, vitamin B12, folate, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, B-vitamin panel, and moisture data for this stir-fried rice noodle dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific dish category as of 2024. 3. Trans fat is reported at 0.04 g per 100 g from the input record; this low value is plausible for a stir-fried dish using standard cooking oil and has been retained. 4. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate based on rice noodles / rice vermicelli from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008); no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found. 5. The 'V' in the dish name (614D Stir-fried Spicy Rice Noodle with V) most likely refers to 'Vegetables' based on the dish description and ingredient profile. 6. Dish is classified as halal based on the input record's dietary tags; this should be verified against the specific vendor's halal certification. 7. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) is the HPB Foodo portal, which is an approved primary source for Singapore dishes.
